title: Nosferatu
description: A classic of German Expressionist cinema, Nosferatu tells the story of Count Orlok, an ancient vampire who terrorizes a small town. Freely adapted from Bram Stoker's Dracula, this shadowy masterpiece defined the horror genre and introduced cinema's most iconic monster.
pd_notes: Published 1922. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: The General
description: Buster Keaton's magnum opus. A Confederate engineer races to recover his stolen locomotive and rescue his beloved in this breathtaking blend of comedy, romance, and genuine Civil War action. Widely considered one of the greatest films ever made.
pd_notes: Published 1926. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Metropolis
description: Fritz Lang's visionary masterpiece set in a dystopian future city where workers toil underground while the elite live in luxury above. A towering achievement of science fiction, Metropolis invented the visual language of sci-fi cinema and remains breathtaking a century later.
pd_notes: Published 1927. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: The Kid
description: Charlie Chaplin's first feature-length film. The Tramp discovers and raises an abandoned child, forming an unbreakable bond. A perfect blend of comedy and deeply moving drama, The Kid remains one of cinema's most beloved films and a showcase of Chaplin's genius.
pd_notes: Published 1921. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: A Trip to the Moon
description: The first science fiction film ever made. Georges Méliès sends a group of astronomers to the moon in a bullet-shaped rocket, where they encounter strange moon inhabitants. A 16-minute cinematic miracle that invented the special effects shot.
pd_notes: Published 1902. Extensively documented as public domain worldwide.

title: The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari
description: The ultimate German Expressionist horror film. A deranged hypnotist controls a somnambulist to carry out his murders in a town filled with twisted, angular sets that seem to mirror the madness within. Revolutionary in design and deeply disturbing, Caligari defined psychological horror.
pd_notes: Published 1920. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Battleship Potemkin
description: Sergei Eisenstein's landmark of Soviet cinema and film theory. The crew of a battleship mutinies against their oppressive Tsarist officers, sparking a broader revolutionary uprising. The Odessa Steps sequence remains one of the most imitated and analyzed scenes in film history.
pd_notes: Published 1925. Soviet government production, public domain in US.

title: Safety Last!
description: Harold Lloyd at his daring best. A small-town boy moves to the city, lands a job at a department store, and to impress his sweetheart arranges for a human fly to scale the building — only to have to do it himself. The clock tower climb is the most iconic stunt in silent comedy.
pd_notes: Published 1923. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: The Phantom of the Opera
description: Lon Chaney's legendary performance as the disfigured musical genius haunting the Paris Opera House. His self-designed makeup for the unmasking scene terrified audiences in 1925 and endures as one of cinema's most shocking reveals. A breathtaking Gothic romance.
pd_notes: Published 1925. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Sherlock Jr.
description: Buster Keaton's most inventive film. A movie projectionist dreams himself into the film he is projecting, becoming a brilliant detective. A dazzling meditation on cinema itself, featuring some of Keaton's most jaw-dropping stunts and a bravura sequence of in-film editing.
pd_notes: Published 1924. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: The Gold Rush
description: Chaplin called it the film he most wanted to be remembered by. The Tramp joins the Klondike Gold Rush, braving starvation, blizzards, and rival prospectors. Features the iconic bread roll dance, the boiled boot dinner, and one of cinema's most perfectly constructed comedy sequences.
pd_notes: Published 1925. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Sunrise: A Song of Two Humans
description: Often called the greatest film of the silent era. A farmer is seduced by a city woman into planning his wife's murder, but as he and his wife journey to the city, something remarkable happens. Won the first Academy Award for Best Actress. Pure cinema at its most poetic.
pd_notes: Published 1927. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Our Hospitality
description: Buster Keaton plays a young man who returns to the South to claim his inheritance, unaware that a blood feud has been ongoing between his family and the local Canfield clan. Features the spectacular Niagara Falls stunt that Keaton performed himself at genuine risk of death.
pd_notes: Published 1923. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Steamboat Bill Jr.
description: Buster Keaton's last independent production features one of cinema's greatest action sequences: a cyclone destroying an entire town. The falling house facade — where a window frame passes over Keaton's standing body with inches to spare — was shot in a single unbroken take with no safety measures.
pd_notes: Published 1928. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Intolerance
description: D.W. Griffith's three-hour epic interweaves four stories across different historical periods to illustrate humanity's eternal struggle with intolerance. The Babylon sequence features production design of a scale never before attempted in cinema — and rarely surpassed since. A flawed but overwhelming achievement.
pd_notes: Published 1916. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: The Great Train Robbery
description: The film that invented the Western and established narrative cinema itself. Edison's 12-minute masterpiece follows a gang of outlaws who rob a train and are hunted down by a posse. The final close-up of a bandit shooting directly at the camera terrified audiences and became cinema's first iconic image.
pd_notes: Published 1903. One of the most well-documented public domain films in existence.

title: Broken Blossoms
description: Lillian Gish gives one of cinema's most devastating performances as a young girl sheltered by a gentle Chinese merchant from her brutal father. Griffith's most intimate and aesthetically refined work, shot in a series of exquisite close-ups that redefined screen acting.
pd_notes: Published 1919. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Way Down East
description: Lillian Gish's greatest dramatic performance. A naive country girl is tricked into a sham marriage, abandoned after giving birth, and cast out into a brutal New England winter. The film's climax — Gish on a real ice floe rushing toward a waterfall — remains one of cinema's most thrilling sequences.
pd_notes: Published 1920. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Within Our Gates
description: The oldest surviving film made by an African American director. Oscar Micheaux's powerful drama follows a Black educator seeking funding for a Southern school while confronting the brutal racial injustices of the era. A vital, angry, and courageous work recovered from near-total oblivion.
pd_notes: Published 1920. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: The Navigator
description: A pampered millionaire and his girlfriend are accidentally set adrift on a massive ocean liner — completely alone. Keaton's most commercially successful film at the time transforms a single location into an inexhaustible comedy machine. The underwater sequence was pioneering for its era.
pd_notes: Published 1924. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Seven Chances
description: A man stands to inherit a fortune if he can get married by 7 PM. After being rejected by every woman he knows, he accidentally advertises his proposal and is chased across town by a mob of would-be brides. The boulder avalanche finale was improvised on location and became one of silent cinema's most spectacular gags.
pd_notes: Published 1925. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: The Birth of a Nation
description: CONTENT WARNING: Contains extreme racial prejudice and glorification of the KKK. Historically significant as the most influential film in cinema's technical development — establishing editing, close-ups, and narrative structure still used today — but deeply racist in content. Studied for its historical and technical impact, not its ideology.
pd_notes: Published 1915. Shown for historical/educational purposes. Content warning displayed to users.

title: College
description: Buster Keaton plays a bookworm who must become a star athlete to win the girl he loves. A joyfully physical comedy that showcases Keaton's extraordinary athletic abilities as he hilariously fails at every sport before the climax demands he actually perform them all at once.
pd_notes: Published 1927. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.

title: Gertie the Dinosaur
description: The first animated character with a distinct personality. Winsor McCay spent over a year drawing 10,000 frames to bring Gertie to life — a gentle brontosaurus who plays, sulks, and responds to her creator's commands. The foundation stone of all character animation that followed.
pd_notes: Published 1914. Extensively documented as public domain worldwide.

title: The Crowd
description: King Vidor's devastating portrait of ordinary American life. A young man arrives in New York City full of ambition and dreams of being someone special — and discovers the grinding, anonymous reality of modern urban existence. Shot on the streets of New York with a handheld camera, it remains achingly relevant.
pd_notes: Published 1928. All US copyrights on pre-1928 works expired January 1, 2024.
